Chamath - What I Read This Week

skippedSources.name: Macro Mornings
skippedSources.reason: Pure promotional — 70% off pre-launch pitch for a paid dashboard, no analytical content beyond a single unexplained +3.1% return figure.

items.source: Goldfinger Capital (robertsinn)
items.title: 2026 is the busiest year of junior mining exploration drilling on record
items.idea: Goldfinger Capital notes 2026 stands to be the busiest year of exploration drilling in junior mining history, concentrated in Ontario, BC, Idaho, Newfoundland, Arizona, Nevada, Colombia, and Chile — with updates running across more than a dozen active watchlist names simultaneously.
items.angle: Junior miner drilling intensity is a leading indicator for the next M&A wave in gold/copper; worth screening names with active programs in these belts ahead of potential re-rating or takeout activity.
items.tweetA: Busiest year of junior mining drilling in the sector's history, clustering in Nevada, Idaho, BC, Newfoundland, Colombia, Chile — the same belts major gold and copper producers have been quietly picking off for reserves. Drill results now, M&A next.
items.tweetB: Nobody's talking about junior mining, but 2026 has more simultaneous drill programs running than any year on record. When capital chases AI, financing costs drop for majors doing quiet reserve-replacement deals in the names nobody's watching. Watch the M&A tape, not the drill results.

charts.desc: No chart in email preview — full post covers a dozen+ names individually, worth opening.

items.source: PaperAlfa - Attack the Week (ATW)
items.title: A 30-year trader's actual thesis: markets are a forced character audit, not a P&L game
items.idea: PaperAlfa argues that money is an outcome, not the purpose, of trading — the real driver for durable investors is self-mastery, since markets expose impatience, ego, fear, and discipline in a way you cannot hide from your own P&L, even if you hide it from other people.
items.angle: Weak on tradeable content but a genuine framework for why most traders blow up psychologically rather than strategically — useful for client conversations about risk-tolerance mismatch and process discipline.
items.tweetA: A trading veteran's actual thesis after nearly three decades: the P&L isn't the point, it's the audit. Markets reveal impatience, ego, and discipline faster than any performance review ever will. Most blowups aren't a strategy problem, they're an identity problem.
items.tweetB: Everyone benchmarks themselves against Druckenmiller or PTJ. The better question is whether you're a better investor than you were last year — compounding applies to process, not just capital, and almost nobody actually tracks it.

charts.desc: No chart — personal essay, calendar/dashboard sections are behind the fold.

items.source: TMAD Weekly
items.title: First monthly CPI deflation since 2020 lands Tuesday — with a fuel-shock tail risk nobody's hedging
items.idea: TMAD flags Tuesday's CPI expected at -0.1% headline (first monthly deflation since 2020) with core still +0.2%, plus Wednesday's PPI expected to fall sharply to +0.1% from 1.1%. Combined with June's weak 57K jobs report and shrinking consumer credit, the setup leans toward rate-cut optimism — unless shipping/strait disruption produces a fuel supply crunch that flips yields higher instead.
items.angle: The real tail risk this week isn't the CPI print, it's that a soft inflation number gets overridden within 48 hours if oil logistics break — positioning for a dovish surprise into Tuesday looks asymmetric against an unhedged fuel-shock wildcard.
items.tweetA: First monthly deflation print since 2020 is coming Tuesday (CPI est. -0.1%), PPI gets cut in half Wednesday, and jobs already missed at 57K. Every ingredient for a dovish Fed pivot is lined up — except the one nobody's hedging: a fuel supply crunch that could flip yields the other way overnight.
items.tweetB: Soft CPI plus soft PPI plus weak jobs equals a rate-cut narrative. But shipping traffic still hasn't recovered even where straits have reopened. If oil logistics break before the Fed meets, this week's 'good' data becomes irrelevant within 48 hours.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the FX book and asset allocation dashboard.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: Verification is emerging as a third scaling axis, alongside pre-training and test-time compute
items.idea: A Stanford/NVIDIA/UC Berkeley collaboration built a training-free verifier that reads a continuous, calibrated score straight off scoring-token logits instead of a discrete pass/fail grade, hitting 86.5% on Terminal-Bench V2, 78.2% on SWE-Bench Verified, 87.4% on RoboRewardBench, and 73.3% on MedAgentBench — and the same score doubles as a live task-progress signal shipped inside a Claude Code extension.
items.angle: Verification-as-third-scaling-axis is the framework worth tracking for where the next capability jump comes from — not bigger models, better graders, and it's already shipping in production tooling, not just papers.
items.tweetA: Everyone's arguing about whether scaling laws are dead. Meanwhile Stanford/NVIDIA just shipped a verifier that reads confidence straight off the logits instead of asking for a pass/fail grade — and it's already live inside Claude Code as a task-progress signal. Verification might be the third scaling axis nobody's pricing.
items.tweetB: 86.5% on coding, 87.4% on robotics, 73% on medical agents — same verifier, zero fine-tuning, across three unrelated domains. The bottleneck in agentic AI was never the policy model. It was always the grader.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the benchmark breakdown.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: A 130-page survey argues 'agent state' is authority and obligations, not memory
items.idea: A new survey reframes durable state in always-on AI agents as task ledgers, permissions, credentials, commitments, provenance, triggers, and already-committed effects — scoring each state item across six axes (authority, scope, mutability, provenance, recoverability, actionability) and tracing a full lifecycle from write through forget, audit, and rollback.
items.angle: As agentic coding/ops tools move toward always-on operation, this becomes the governance framework enterprises will need before granting agents standing permissions — a product category (agent state auditing) that doesn't really exist yet.
items.tweetA: There's no vendor selling 'agent state auditing' yet, and there will be. A new survey reframes AI agent memory as authority plus obligations plus committed effects — the stuff that makes an agent consequential, not just conversational. That's a compliance product waiting to be built.
items.tweetB: The AI safety conversation is stuck on hallucinations. The actual risk with always-on agents is state: credentials it holds, commitments it already made on your behalf, effects it can't take back. Nobody's auditing that yet.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the six-axis scoring framework.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: HOLA: a 340M-parameter linear-attention model just beat full attention on perplexity
items.idea: HOLA pairs a compressive recurrent state with a small, learning-free exact KV cache for linear-attention models, cutting Wikitext perplexity from 27.32 to 22.92 at 340M parameters — beating a full-attention Transformer++ baseline at 26.88 — while staying robust on needle-in-haystack recall out to 32k tokens, 16x its training length.
items.angle: A cheap linear-attention model beating full attention on perplexity at small scale suggests the linear-attention camp is closing the quality gap while keeping its cost advantage — worth watching for who licenses this into a production model next.
items.tweetA: A 340M linear-attention model just beat a full-attention Transformer on perplexity — 22.92 vs 26.88 — by keeping a tiny exact cache alongside the compressed state. Efficient attention was supposed to trade quality for speed. That trade might be closing.
items.tweetB: 16x length generalization on recall tasks, from a model that isn't even paying full attention's memory cost. If this holds at scale, the 'quadratic attention is the price of long context' assumption gets a lot shakier.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the perplexity and recall benchmark tables.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: NVIDIA doubles server throughput on the same hardware via Puzzle-75B compression
items.idea: NVIDIA compressed the hybrid MoE Nemotron-3-Super into Puzzle-75B-A9B, roughly doubling interactive server throughput on a single 8xB200 node while holding quality across reasoning, coding, long-context, and agentic benchmarks — and lifting concurrency at 1M-token context from 1 request to 8 on a single H100.
items.angle: This is a direct capex-efficiency story — if serving costs halve at matched quality, the 'AI is unprofitable per query' bear case gets weaker every quarter compression research like this ships. Worth tracking against NVDA's own margin commentary.
items.tweetA: NVIDIA just showed you can double server throughput on the same 8xB200 node without losing accuracy on reasoning or coding benchmarks. Every compute-margin bear case for AI assumes cost curves are flat. They're not — they're compounding.
items.tweetB: 1M-token context, 8 concurrent requests on a single H100, up from 1. That's not a benchmark win, that's a unit-economics rewrite for anyone serving long-context agents in production.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the throughput-vs-quality curves.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: The orchestration harness moved cost per task more than switching between six frontier models
items.idea: A study held six foundation models fixed (Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1, Qwen 3.6, GLM 5.1, and others) and varied only the orchestration harness across 22 tasks — cutting blended cost per task 41%, tokens per task 38%, and median wall-clock 44%, with harness-driven quality gains correlating almost perfectly (r=0.99) with the underlying model's baseline strength.
items.angle: This undercuts the 'just wait for the next flagship model' framing — the orchestration layer moved cost per task more than the entire spread of models tested, meaning the real near-term alpha in enterprise AI deployment is harness engineering, not model selection.
items.tweetA: A study held the model constant and only changed the orchestration layer. Result: cost per task dropped 41%, tokens dropped 38%, latency dropped 44% — bigger swings than switching between six different frontier models. Everyone's optimizing model choice. The harness was the lever.
items.tweetB: r=0.99 correlation between harness-driven quality gains and baseline model strength. Translation: a better harness doesn't just save money, it makes your best model even better relative to weaker ones. That's a moat, not a feature.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — likely a cost/tokens/latency comparison across all six models and harness configurations.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: ReContext: models with 128K context windows still can't find evidence already in the prompt
items.idea: ReContext is a training-free inference harness that treats long context as a memory store and the query as a retrieval cue, improving evidence utilization across Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and Llama3-8B on eight 128K-context datasets without any fine-tuning or pruning of the original context.
items.angle: Another data point that 'just give it a bigger context window' doesn't solve retrieval — the fix keeps coming from inference-time tricks, not raw window size, which matters for anyone evaluating 1M+ token context claims from vendors.
items.tweetA: 128K context windows and models still can't find evidence that's already sitting in the prompt. The fix isn't a bigger window, it's a retrieval cue layered on top at inference time, with zero training. Context length was never the bottleneck; attention allocation was.
items.tweetB: If you're benchmarking 'long context' models on whether they can retrieve a needle, you're testing the wrong thing. This paper shows treating context as memory beats brute-force window size across eight different long-context datasets.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the per-dataset evidence-utilization results.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: Oxford taxonomy: the same six agent failure modes keep reappearing, and scaffolding doesn't fix them
items.idea: Oxford synthesized 27 benchmark, taxonomy, and audit papers spanning 19 benchmarks into six recurring LLM-agent failure clusters — tool errors, planning failures, long-horizon context degradation, multi-agent coordination breakdowns, adversarial safety failures, and measurement validity problems — finding reliability drops faster than task length grows, and that adding scaffolding does not reliably improve it.
items.angle: 'Just add more scaffolding' is the default enterprise response to agent failures right now — this taxonomy is direct evidence that's often wrong, which matters for anyone pricing in near-term 'agents replace headcount' timelines.
items.tweetA: Oxford went through 27 papers and found the same six agent failure modes keep reappearing across totally unrelated benchmarks — and scaffolding doesn't reliably fix any of them. The 'just add more orchestration' fix everyone's shipping might not be a fix.
items.tweetB: Reliability drops faster than task length grows. That's the whole problem with 'agents will replace X headcount by 2027' timelines — sub-task benchmark scores don't compound the way multi-step production workflows need them to.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the six-cluster taxonomy breakdown.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: A 0.6B retriever generalizes 10x past its training length on million-token corpora
items.idea: BlockSearch runs the first systematic study of in-context retrieval at real retriever scale (million-token corpora), introducing a 0.6B parameter language-model retriever that improves over prior LM baselines and length-generalizes up to 10 times beyond its training length.
items.angle: Small, cheap retrievers that generalize far past training length matter for cost — you don't need a frontier model's giant context window if a 0.6B retriever can pre-filter a million-token corpus first.
items.tweetA: A 0.6B parameter retriever that generalizes 10x past its training length on million-token corpora. This is the unglamorous infrastructure that makes 'chat with your entire codebase' actually affordable instead of just a demo.
items.tweetB: Nobody needs a frontier model with a 10M-token context window if a cheap retriever can find the right million tokens first. Retrieval is quietly doing the job that context-window marketing gets the credit for.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the length-generalization curves.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: MIT fixes RLVR's reward-hacking problem by adding a 'does this look human' discriminator
items.idea: MIT added an adversarial discriminator trained on human demonstrations as a human-likeness proxy alongside task-accuracy reward in RLVR training — preserving RLVR's accuracy gains on bug fixing and story generation while nearly eliminating the reward hacking and style collapse that normally emerges when optimizing only for verifiable reward.
items.angle: This directly answers the 'RL post-training makes model outputs weird and robotic' complaint that's been circulating — worth watching whether labs adopt dual-reward setups more broadly given reward hacking is the most cited RLVR failure mode.
items.tweetA: RL with verifiable rewards quietly makes models write like robots and hack the reward function. MIT's fix: add a discriminator that scores 'does this look human,' and reward hacking nearly disappears without giving up the accuracy gains. Simple idea, underused so far.
items.tweetB: The dirty secret of RLVR post-training is that optimizing for objectively-scoreable output destroys style and invites reward hacking. This is the first fix that keeps both the accuracy gains and the writing quality.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the reward-hacking benchmark comparison.

items.source: NLP News - Top AI Papers of the Week (Elvis Saravia)
items.title: A coding agent replicated four ML papers end to end — but its own process wasn't reproducible
items.idea: A coding agent replicated ML papers from materials alone across 12 runs over 4 papers, passing a workspace-evidence completion gate and matching all 158 recorded claim targets in every run — but repeated runs differed in how they split claims into targets and in numerical fidelity, meaning completion was reproducible even when the path to it wasn't.
items.angle: 'AI agents can now reproduce published ML research' is a bigger claim than it sounds — this is early evidence toward agent-run science, but the non-reproducibility of the agent's own method (not just the science) is the part worth flagging to anyone citing this as proof agents can do R&D reliably.
items.tweetA: A coding agent replicated four ML papers end to end and matched all 158 claims across 12 runs. The catch: the agent's own process wasn't reproducible — it split up the claims differently every time. Reliable output, unreliable method. That's a strange kind of progress.
items.tweetB: 'AI agents can now replicate published research' sounds like a headline. The real finding is narrower: completion is reproducible even when the path isn't — exactly the failure mode you'd worry about if agents start doing unsupervised science.

charts.desc: No chart — methodology paper.

items.source: Big Moves (Big Price Moves Explained)
items.title: SK Hynix raises $26.5bn and walks straight into forced Nasdaq-100 buying
items.idea: SK Hynix raised $26.5bn in a U.S. capital raise Friday and will list ADRs on Nasdaq, likely joining the Nasdaq-100 within weeks. Bulls cite ~2x YoY revenue growth, 4x net income growth, 70%+ operating margins, and an order book sold out through the end of next year; bears note much of that order book is backed by customers who themselves need to raise capital to fund the spending.
items.angle: A memory chipmaker doing a $26.5bn raise to fund AI capacity, immediately headed for passive-index inclusion, is close to textbook late-cycle bubble mechanics — forced index-fund buying regardless of valuation.
items.tweetA: SK Hynix raises $26.5B and walks straight into Nasdaq-100 inclusion within weeks. That means index funds become forced buyers at whatever price, no valuation opinion required. Bubbles don't need conviction once passive flows take over — they just need index membership.
items.tweetB: The bull case for SK Hynix is a sold-out order book through 2027. The bear case is that a chunk of those customers are themselves raising capital to pay for the memory they've ordered. That's circular financing, and circular financing is how every bubble funds its own denominator.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the revenue/margin trajectory.

items.source: Big Moves (Big Price Moves Explained)
items.title: Meta's 'Iris' chip plan is really a bet on giving AI away free to 4 billion users
items.idea: Meta is reportedly building its own AI accelerator (code-named Iris) with Broadcom/TSMC, alongside AI capex of roughly $135bn this year (nearly double 2025), aiming to cut inference cost per token/image while Muse Image and Muse Spark get embedded directly into Instagram, WhatsApp, and Advantage Plus ad tools. Shares rose 6-9% intraday on the news.
items.angle: The market is pricing Meta's chip move as margin expansion, but the real tell is the freemium-to-subscription funnel (Meta One) — Meta needs the Iris cost cuts to make free-tier AI image generation sustainable at billions of users, a much bigger volume bet than anything OpenAI or Anthropic is running.
items.tweetA: Meta's $135B AI capex story usually gets framed as an arms race. The actual bet is different: Iris chips need to make image generation cheap enough to give away free to billions of Instagram and WhatsApp users and still fund a subscription upsell. That's a volume game none of the API-first labs are playing.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching NVDA for AI capex signals. Meanwhile Meta just told you it's trying to need NVDA less — own model, own chip, own distribution to 4 billion users. If Iris ships on schedule, that's the most vertically integrated AI stack outside of Google.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the capex and margin trajectory detail.

items.source: Big Moves (Big Price Moves Explained)
items.title: Barnes & Noble Education's first-ever dividend lands the same quarter it missed revenue estimates
items.idea: Barnes & Noble Education swung to $16.9M net income from a $65.8M prior-year loss, cut net debt ~33% to $62.6M, grew its First Day textbook-access program 28% to $760M (now covering 1.25M students, guided to 1.4M by fall), and initiated its first-ever dividend ($0.08/quarter) — while still missing revenue estimates ($267.1M vs $295.5M expected) and guiding fiscal 2027 EBITDA of just $85-92M.
items.angle: A distressed campus retailer converting a subscription-style textbook-access program into recurring institutional revenue is a small-cap turnaround pattern worth flagging, but the same-quarter revenue miss is the reminder that turnarounds re-rate slower than the good-news headlines suggest.
items.tweetA: Barnes & Noble Education just posted its first dividend ever after cutting net debt a third and flipping from a $65.8M loss to $16.9M net income. The stock still fell 2% on the print because revenue missed. Turnarounds get priced on the miss, not the trend, every time.
items.tweetB: 1.25 million students now on First Day Complete, guided to 1.4M by fall. That's a subscription-style textbook model quietly compounding inside a company most people still think is a bankrupt bookstore chain.

charts.desc: No chart in email — earnings tables in the linked post.

items.source: Chamath - What I Read This Week
items.title: Meta shipped its first-ever paid closed model, abandoning the open-weight strategy mid price war
items.idea: Three frontier labs shipped new models within a day of each other: Grok 4.5, GPT-5.6 (as Sol/Terra/Luna), and Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 — Meta's first paid closed model. Pricing: Grok 4.5 at $2/$6 per million tokens, OpenAI's Luna at $1/$6, Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 at $1.25/$4.25, versus Anthropic's Opus 4.8 at $25 output, OpenAI's Sol at $30, and Anthropic's Fable 5 at $50 output.
items.angle: Meta abandoning open-weights for its first paid closed model is the bigger signal than the pricing race itself — the open-source 'give away the model, monetize the app' strategy just lost its biggest backer, right as three labs converge on tool-use/agentic capability as the differentiator instead of raw benchmarks.
items.tweetA: Meta just shipped its first-ever paid closed model. The company that spent three years positioning open-weights as a strategic weapon against OpenAI just switched sides. If Meta doesn't believe open-source wins anymore, that's a bigger tell than any benchmark in this week's model releases.
items.tweetB: $1-2 per million input tokens for three frontier-adjacent models this week, versus $25-50 for the actual flagships. That's not a price war, that's market segmentation: cheap models for agentic tool-calling at volume, expensive models for the reasoning tasks that still need them.

charts.desc: No chart — pricing comparison in text.

items.source: Chamath - What I Read This Week
items.title: DeepSeek is designing its own inference chip — the fourth Chinese lab to do so
items.idea: Reuters reported July 7 that DeepSeek is designing its own AI inference chip to cut reliance on Nvidia and Huawei — a roughly year-old effort now in talks with chip designers, foundries, and memory suppliers, joining Alibaba, Baidu, and Zhipu AI in pursuing custom silicon, while still needing TSMC/Samsung/SK Hynix approval under U.S. export rules for both leading-edge fabrication and HBM memory.
items.angle: China's AI ecosystem is fragmenting into at least four separate custom-chip efforts rather than consolidating around Huawei — a sign the export-control chokepoint (HBM plus advanced fab access) is binding hard enough that every major Chinese AI lab thinks it needs its own hardware hedge, not that any one of them expects to win outright.
items.tweetA: Four separate Chinese AI labs are now designing their own chips — DeepSeek, Alibaba, Baidu, Zhipu — instead of consolidating behind Huawei. That's not confidence, that's every lab independently concluding it can't trust one national champion to solve the export-control chokepoint for all of them.
items.tweetB: DeepSeek can design an inference chip in a garage. It cannot design its way around needing TSMC or Samsung sign-off for the HBM memory that chip needs to actually run fast. The bottleneck was never silicon design, it's still packaging and memory.

charts.desc: No chart — news summary with links to source reporting.

items.source: Chamath - What I Read This Week
items.title: Xbox is running a 3% margin because Gen Z stopped paying for games entirely, not just because of Game Pass
items.idea: Microsoft cut 4,800 jobs (2.1% of workforce), mostly Xbox, spinning out four gaming studios, with the division tracking to a ~3% margin (vs Microsoft's company-wide ~39%) — driven by Game Pass cannibalizing $70 purchases with a $20/month subscription, console hardware sales down 33% YoY, and buyers aged 18-24 spending ~25% less on games than 2024 while shifting hours to free platforms like Roblox and Fortnite.
items.angle: This is a demographic spending-substitution story as much as a corporate-strategy failure — the cohort that used to buy $70 titles is spending its discretionary gaming dollars nowhere at all, which is a harder problem for Game Pass pricing to fix than a subscription-cannibalization narrative alone suggests.
items.tweetA: Xbox is running a 3% margin against Microsoft's 39% company-wide number, and the real culprit isn't Game Pass cannibalization, it's that 18-24 year olds are spending 25% less on games entirely while playing more hours on free platforms like Roblox and Fortnite. You can't subscription-price your way out of a demographic that stopped paying altogether.
items.tweetB: Microsoft is the worst-performing Mag 7 stock this year, down 18%+, and just cut 4,800 jobs mostly from Xbox. Notice who's absorbing the loss: four studios get spun back out as independents, meaning Microsoft offloads the content-creation risk while keeping the platform. That's re-architecting who eats the downside, not retreat.

charts.desc: No chart — figures cited in text.

items.source: Young and Calculated
items.title: Jane Street sits on $45bn of its own capital and still borrows at 6.75%+ rather than dilute partners
items.idea: Jane Street holds roughly $45bn of members' equity with zero outside shareholders, generated $39.6bn trading revenue in 2025 (beating JPMorgan's trading revenue by ~11%) on just 3,500 employees (~$2.7m average comp per employee firmwide), yet still issues bonds at 6.75%+ and above despite having $45bn in-house — because diluting partner equity is treated internally as more expensive than debt.
items.angle: A private partnership choosing to borrow at 6.75%+ rather than dilute internal equity is a pure revealed-preference signal of how partners privately price their own firm — probably well north of what any outside GP-stakes buyer would offer, which is exactly why Jane Street, unlike Millennium, has never taken outside capital.
items.tweetA: Jane Street sits on $45bn of its own capital and still borrows at 6.75%+ rather than issue more partner equity. That's not a liquidity choice, that's partners telling you exactly how expensive they think their own equity is, probably far above whatever a Millennium-style outside stake would pay.
items.tweetB: $39.6bn in trading revenue beats JPMorgan's trading desk by 11%, with 3,500 people instead of tens of thousands. Average pay across the entire firm, not just traders, is $2.7M. That's the actual scoreboard for why quant partnerships never go public.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the members'-equity growth trajectory (2016-2026).

items.source: Young and Calculated
items.title: Citadel PMs own nothing when they leave; Jane Street members are unwinding real equity
items.idea: Ken Griffin owns ~85% of Citadel, with Sequoia and Paradigm holding the only outside stake ($1.15bn for ~5% at a $22bn valuation in 2022). Citadel funds itself with $67bn of investor AUM plus leveraged loans rather than employee equity, meaning PMs are paid off deferred-comp schedules tied to their own trailing P&L, not a claim on the enterprise — the structural opposite of Jane Street's partnership model.
items.angle: The practical consequence for anyone choosing where to work in quant finance: a Citadel PM who quits walks away from a deferred comp schedule that stops compounding, while a Jane Street member who quits is unwinding an actual equity stake — structurally different bets on the same career headline comp numbers.
items.tweetA: Two of the biggest names in quant finance, opposite ownership models: Citadel PMs get paid off their own trailing P&L and own nothing when they leave. Jane Street members are unwinding real equity. Same industry, same comp headlines, completely different asset when you walk out the door.
items.tweetB: Griffin owns 85% of Citadel and has never sold control, only 5% ever went outside, to Sequoia and Paradigm, at a $22bn mark in 2022. If that stake traded today given Citadel's scale, it's a very different number, and Griffin knows it, which is exactly why he hasn't sold more.

charts.desc: Worth opening for the debt-stack comparison across firms.

items.source: Young and Calculated
items.title: Millennium sold 15% of itself for $2bn — the first outside equity in the firm's 35-year history
items.idea: In late 2025, Millennium sold roughly 15% of its management company for about $2bn, implying a $14bn valuation, the first outside equity event in the firm's 35-year history — following the same pattern as Bridgewater's Brunei Investment Agency stake and D.E. Shaw/AQR's earlier hybrid structures.
items.angle: This is the clearest data point yet that GP-stakes buyers now value multi-strategy management companies richer than the funds they run — institutional capital is rotating from 'buy the fund's returns' to 'buy the fee stream itself,' the same trade private equity made with its own GPs a decade earlier.
items.tweetA: Millennium just sold 15% of itself for $2bn, a $14bn mark on the management company, not the fund. First outside capital in 35 years. Institutional money isn't trying to access Millennium's returns anymore, it's trying to own the fee stream. That's the same trade PE made with its own GP stakes ten years ago, just arriving in multi-strat now.
items.tweetB: Four ownership structures, one industry: Jane Street (0% outside), Citadel (5% outside, still Griffin-controlled), Bridgewater (founder fully exited, employee-owned), Millennium (15% sold at $14bn). The dispersion in who's selling and who isn't tells you more about where the two-and-twenty era is heading than any single fund's returns this year.

charts.desc: 🔥 Worth opening for the four-firm ownership-structure comparison table.
